tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • randlc: 4 occurrences<>
  • vranlc: 3 occurrences<>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • ADD: 1 occurrences<>
  • INC: 1 occurrences<>
  • LEA: 1 occurrences<>

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 14.83 cycles. At this rate:
  • 10% of peak load performance is reached (10.25 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 6% of peak store performance is reached (4.31 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 14.83 to 12.33 cycles (1.20x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 48.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 14.83 to 3.33 cycles (4.45x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 10% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 14.83 to 1.96 cycles (7.57x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

header:
The loop is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2163-2170,2306. Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. Rerun with max-paths=10
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option. This loop has 10 execution paths. The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck. Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX
Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)
